macro_segments.title: Opening: Review of Damage Categories and Introduction to Braisa Lists

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and daf orientation
Review of Mishna explanations: Shor, Bor, Ma'aveh, Hev'er and animal liability
Introduction to expanded lists and Rabbi Oshaya's thirteen categories

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Chiya's Expanded List of Twenty-Four Categories and Conceptual Distinctions

micro_segments.title: Enumeration of Rabbi Chiya's twenty-four avot nezikin including theft, false witnesses, and personal injury cases
Analysis of why Rabbi Oshaya did not include certain categories: kanasim vs. mamona distinction

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Compensation and Fines: Defining Mamona and Knas

micro_segments.title: Analysis of false witnesses (Eidim Zomemim): whether compensation or fine, and the role of actual damage
Rashi's explanation of the criteria for distinguishing compensation from arbitrary fines
Tosafot's perspective on Eidim Zomemim and the requirement for actual loss in compensatory damages

macro_segments.title: Extension of Categories: Rape, Seduction, Libel and Non-Visible Damages

micro_segments.title: Personal injury payments in rape and seduction cases and their relationship to Rabbi Oshaya's list
Metameh, Medama, and Menasech: damages that are not visible but constitute compensation
Discussion of whether non-visible damages constitute real damage or rabbinic fine

macro_segments.title: Purpose of the Lists: What Conceptual Distinctions Are Excluded and Why

micro_segments.title: Examination of why certain categories like Moser and Mefagel are excluded despite payment obligations
The role of words versus action in damage causation: libel, false testimony, and informing

macro_segments.title: The Meaning of Avot: Payment from Best Property and Linking of Categories

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Abbahu's explanation: all categories function like the four classic avot to require payment from best property
Deriving the best-property rule from key terminology: tachas, nesina, yishalem, kessef across all categories

macro_segments.title: Why Four Categories Are Needed: Distinctions That Cannot Be Learned Out

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's assertion that one cannot learn all categories from fewer than four due to distinct features
Rava's analysis: demonstrating that Bor plus one other category suffices for most derivations except Keren
Keren as the exception: why goring cannot be derived from other categories and the debate over its nature

macro_segments.title: The Debate Over Keren's Characterization: Negligence, Propensity, and Legal Status

micro_segments.title: The question of whether Keren is fundamentally below the line of expected liability or deserving of full payment
Examination of whether Keren represents kanassa (fine) or mamona (compensation) depending on the underlying assumption

macro_segments.title: Unique Laws Applied to Each of the Four Categories

micro_segments.title: Keren: the half-damage rule and distinction between tam and muad over three instances
Shen and Regel: exemption in reshut harabim and the doctrine of pechats hanizek
Bor: exemptions for vessels and persons, and difference between Rabbi Yehuda's view
Adam: the inclusion of four additional payments for personal injury
Eish: exemption for buried objects and exposure requirement; charring versus consumption

macro_segments.title: Bridging Concrete Categories and Abstract Principles: The Challenge of Categorizing New Cases

micro_segments.title: Summary of distinctive laws for each category and the question of how to apply them to cases that fit none precisely
Preview of next discussion: the abstract rule 'all that is its nature to damage and watching is upon you' and its application
